nodes.standard_name: Kampuchean United Front for National Salvation

nodes.standard_name: Patriotic and Democratic Front of the Great National Union of Kampuchea
nodes.original_text_name: Patriotic and Democratic Front of the Great National Union of Kampuchea
nodes.context: Front formed by the Khmer Rouge in 1979 to broaden its support base.

edges.evidence_quote: The Kampuchea Revolutionary Army was slowly built up in the forests of eastern Cambodia during the late 1960s, supported by the People's Army of Vietnam
edges.context: The People's Army of Vietnam supported the Khmer Rouge's military buildup.

edges.evidence_quote: The Kampuchea Revolutionary Army was slowly built up in the forests of eastern Cambodia during the late 1960s, supported by the ... Viet Cong
edges.context: The Viet Cong supported the Khmer Rouge's military buildup.

edges.evidence_quote: The Kampuchea Revolutionary Army was slowly built up in the forests of eastern Cambodia during the late 1960s, supported by the ... Pathet Lao
edges.context: The Pathet Lao supported the Khmer Rouge's military buildup.

edges.evidence_quote: In the 1970s, the Khmer Rouge was largely supported and funded by the CCP
edges.context: The Chinese Communist Party supported and funded the Khmer Rouge.

edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Cambodian Civil War: GRUNK
edges.context: GRUNK was an ally of the Khmer Rouge during the Cambodian Civil War.

edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Cambodian–Vietnamese War: FUNCINPEC
edges.context: FUNCINPEC was an ally of the Khmer Rouge during the Cambodian-Vietnamese War.

edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Cambodian–Vietnamese War: KPNLF
edges.context: KPNLF was an ally of the Khmer Rouge during the Cambodian-Vietnamese War.

edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Cambodian Civil War: Communist Party of Thailand
edges.context: The Communist Party of Thailand was an ally of the Khmer Rouge during the Cambodian Civil War.

edges.evidence_quote: The regime was removed from power in 1979 when Vietnam invaded Cambodia and quickly destroyed most of its forces.
edges.context: Vietnam invaded Cambodia and fought against the Khmer Rouge, removing them from power.

edges.evidence_quote: Opponents: Cambodian–Vietnamese War: FUNSK (1978–1979)
edges.context: FUNSK opposed the Khmer Rouge during the Cambodian-Vietnamese War.

edges.evidence_quote: Opponents: Cambodian–Vietnamese War: People's Republic of Kampuchea (1979–1989)
edges.context: The People's Republic of Kampuchea opposed the Khmer Rouge from 1979 to 1989.

edges.evidence_quote: Opponents: Cambodian–Vietnamese War: State of Cambodia (1989–1992)
edges.context: The State of Cambodia opposed the Khmer Rouge from 1989 to 1992.

edges.evidence_quote: The Kampuchea Revolutionary Army was slowly built up in the forests of eastern Cambodia during the late 1960s, supported by the People's Army of Vietnam
edges.context: The People's Army of Vietnam supported the Khmer Rouge's military buildup.

edges.evidence_quote: The Kampuchea Revolutionary Army was slowly built up in the forests of eastern Cambodia during the late 1960s, supported by the ... Viet Cong
edges.context: The Viet Cong supported the Khmer Rouge's military buildup.

edges.evidence_quote: The Kampuchea Revolutionary Army was slowly built up in the forests of eastern Cambodia during the late 1960s, supported by the ... Pathet Lao
edges.context: The Pathet Lao supported the Khmer Rouge's military buildup.

edges.evidence_quote: The Kampuchea Revolutionary Army was slowly built up in the forests of eastern Cambodia during the late 1960s, supported by the ... Chinese Communist Party (CCP).
edges.context: The Chinese Communist Party supported the Khmer Rouge's military buildup.

edges.evidence_quote: The history of the communist movement in Cambodia can be divided into six phases, namely the emergence before World War II of the Indochinese Communist Party (ICP)
edges.context: The Khmer Rouge emerged from the broader Indochinese communist movement, with the ICP as a predecessor.

edges.evidence_quote: the 10-year struggle for independence from the French, when a separate Cambodian communist party, the Kampuchean People's Revolutionary Party (KPRP), was established under Vietnamese auspices
edges.context: The KPRP was a predecessor to the Khmer Rouge, which later split from it.

edges.evidence_quote: Viet Minh units occasionally made forays into Cambodian bases during their war against the French and in conjunction with the leftist government that ruled Thailand until 1947. The Viet Minh encouraged the formation of armed, left-wing Khmer Issarak bands.
edges.context: The Viet Minh supported Khmer Issarak groups, which later contributed to the Khmer Rouge.

edges.evidence_quote: The Viet Minh encouraged the formation of armed, left-wing Khmer Issarak bands.
edges.context: Khmer Issarak groups were precursors to the Khmer Rouge, with some members later joining the communist movement.

edges.evidence_quote: On 17 April 1950, the first nationwide congress of the Khmer Issarak groups convened, and the United Issarak Front was established.
edges.context: The United Issarak Front was a coalition of Khmer Issarak groups, which later evolved into the Khmer Rouge.

edges.evidence_quote: In 1951, the ICP was reorganized into three national units, namely the Vietnam Workers' Party, the Lao Issara, and the Kampuchean or Khmer People's Revolutionary Party (KPRP). According to a document issued after the reorganization, the Vietnam Workers' Party would continue to 'supervise' the smaller Laotian and Cambodian movements.
edges.context: The Vietnam Workers' Party supervised the KPRP, which was a predecessor of the Khmer Rouge.

edges.evidence_quote: In 1951, the ICP was reorganized into three national units, namely the Vietnam Workers' Party, the Lao Issara, and the Kampuchean or Khmer People's Revolutionary Party (KPRP).
edges.context: The Lao Issara was a sister party to the KPRP under the ICP reorganization.

edges.evidence_quote: In late 1954, those who stayed in Cambodia founded a legal political party, the Pracheachon
edges.context: The Pracheachon was a legal front for the KPRP, which was a predecessor of the Khmer Rouge.

edges.evidence_quote: Members of the Pracheachon were subject to harassment and arrests because the party remained outside Sihanouk's political organization, Sangkum.
edges.context: Sangkum was a rival political organization to the Pracheachon, which was linked to the Khmer Rouge.

edges.evidence_quote: At some time between 1949 and 1951, Pol Pot and Ieng Sary joined the French Communist Party.
edges.context: Key Khmer Rouge leaders were members of the French Communist Party.

edges.evidence_quote: They transformed the Khmer Students Association (KSA), to which most of the 200 or so Khmer students in Paris belonged, into an organization for nationalist and leftist ideas.
edges.context: The KSA was transformed by future Khmer Rouge leaders into a leftist organization.

edges.evidence_quote: Inside the KSA and its successor organizations, there was a secret organization known as the Cercle Marxiste (Marxist circle).
edges.context: The Cercle Marxiste was a secret Marxist group within the KSA, which later became part of the Khmer Rouge.

edges.evidence_quote: a year later, the French authorities closed down the KSA, but Hou Yuon and Khieu Samphan helped to establish in 1956 a new group, the Khmer Students Union.
edges.context: The Khmer Students Union succeeded the KSA, both linked to the Khmer Rouge.

edges.evidence_quote: The Kampuchea Revolutionary Army was slowly built up in the forests of eastern Cambodia during the late 1960s, supported by the People's Army of Vietnam
edges.context: The People's Army of Vietnam supported the Khmer Rouge's military buildup in the late 1960s.

edges.evidence_quote: The Kampuchea Revolutionary Army was slowly built up in the forests of eastern Cambodia during the late 1960s, supported by the ... Viet Cong
edges.context: The Viet Cong supported the Khmer Rouge's military buildup in the late 1960s.

edges.evidence_quote: The Kampuchea Revolutionary Army was slowly built up in the forests of eastern Cambodia during the late 1960s, supported by the ... Pathet Lao
edges.context: The Pathet Lao supported the Khmer Rouge's military buildup in the late 1960s.

edges.evidence_quote: the CCP largely 'armed and trained' the Khmer Rouge, including Pol Pot, both during the Cambodian Civil War and the years afterward.
edges.context: The Chinese Communist Party armed and trained the Khmer Rouge during and after the civil war.

edges.evidence_quote: Sihanouk made an alliance with the Khmer Rouge on the advice of CCP, and became the nominal head of a Khmer Rouge–dominated government-in-exile (known by its French acronym GRUNK) backed by China.
edges.context: The Khmer Rouge dominated the GRUNK government-in-exile, with Sihanouk as nominal head.

edges.evidence_quote: The Kampuchea Revolutionary Army was slowly built up in the forests of eastern Cambodia during the late 1960s, supported by the People's Army of Vietnam
edges.context: The People's Army of Vietnam supported the Khmer Rouge in building its army.

edges.evidence_quote: The Kampuchea Revolutionary Army was slowly built up in the forests of eastern Cambodia during the late 1960s, supported by the People's Army of Vietnam, the Viet Cong
edges.context: The Viet Cong supported the Khmer Rouge in building its army.

edges.evidence_quote: The Kampuchea Revolutionary Army was slowly built up in the forests of eastern Cambodia during the late 1960s, supported by the People's Army of Vietnam, the Viet Cong, the Pathet Lao
edges.context: The Pathet Lao supported the Khmer Rouge in building its army.

edges.evidence_quote: The Kampuchea Revolutionary Army was slowly built up in the forests of eastern Cambodia during the late 1960s, supported by the People's Army of Vietnam, the Viet Cong, the Pathet Lao, and the Chinese Communist Party (CCP).
edges.context: The Chinese Communist Party supported the Khmer Rouge in building its army and later advised them.

edges.evidence_quote: The Kampuchea Revolutionary Army was slowly built up in the forests of eastern Cambodia during the late 1960s, supported by the People's Army of Vietnam
edges.context: The People's Army of Vietnam supported the Khmer Rouge in building its army.

edges.evidence_quote: The Kampuchea Revolutionary Army was slowly built up in the forests of eastern Cambodia during the late 1960s, supported by the ... Viet Cong
edges.context: The Viet Cong supported the Khmer Rouge in building its army.

edges.evidence_quote: The Kampuchea Revolutionary Army was slowly built up in the forests of eastern Cambodia during the late 1960s, supported by the ... Pathet Lao
edges.context: The Pathet Lao supported the Khmer Rouge in building its army.

edges.evidence_quote: The Kampuchea Revolutionary Army was slowly built up in the forests of eastern Cambodia during the late 1960s, supported by the ... Chinese Communist Party (CCP).
edges.context: The Chinese Communist Party supported the Khmer Rouge in building its army and later provided military aid.

edges.target: Kampuchean United Front for National Salvation
edges.evidence_quote: the Vietnamese armed forces along with the Kampuchean United Front for National Salvation, an organization founded by Heng Samrin that included many dissatisfied former Khmer Rouge members, invaded Cambodia and captured Phnom Penh on 7 January 1979.
edges.context: The Kampuchean United Front for National Salvation, composed of former Khmer Rouge members, fought against the Khmer Rouge alongside Vietnam.

edges.evidence_quote: China, the United States and the ASEAN countries sponsored the creation and the military operations of a Cambodian government in exile, known as the Coalition Government of Democratic Kampuchea, which included the Khmer Rouge, the republican Khmer People's National Liberation Front and the royalist Funcinpec Party.
edges.context: The Khmer Rouge was a member of the Coalition Government of Democratic Kampuchea.

edges.evidence_quote: China, the United States and the ASEAN countries sponsored the creation and the military operations of a Cambodian government in exile, known as the Coalition Government of Democratic Kampuchea, which included the Khmer Rouge, the republican Khmer People's National Liberation Front and the royalist Funcinpec Party.
edges.context: The Khmer Rouge and the Khmer People's National Liberation Front were both members of the Coalition Government of Democratic Kampuchea.

edges.evidence_quote: China, the United States and the ASEAN countries sponsored the creation and the military operations of a Cambodian government in exile, known as the Coalition Government of Democratic Kampuchea, which included the Khmer Rouge, the republican Khmer People's National Liberation Front and the royalist Funcinpec Party.
edges.context: The Khmer Rouge and Funcinpec Party were both members of the Coalition Government of Democratic Kampuchea.

edges.target: Patriotic and Democratic Front of the Great National Union of Kampuchea
edges.evidence_quote: In an attempt to broaden its support base, the Khmer Rouge formed the Patriotic and Democratic Front of the Great National Union of Kampuchea in 1979.
edges.context: The Khmer Rouge created the Patriotic and Democratic Front of the Great National Union of Kampuchea.
